name: GitHub Copilot Extensions API
what_it_does: API for building custom GitHub Copilot Extensions — chat agents accessible inside GitHub Copilot Chat in VS Code, JetBrains, and GitHub.com that can respond with GitHub-contextual answers, call external APIs, and return rich content.
use_cases: Building internal tooling agents accessible to developers directly inside their IDE via @extension-name syntax
Integrating third-party services (Jira, Datadog, PagerDuty) into Copilot Chat so developers never leave their editor
Creating custom code reviewers or security scanners that surface results inside Copilot Chat
Exposing internal documentation or runbook search as a Copilot agent for developer self-service
Wrapping organization-specific LLM workflows as first-class Copilot Chat participants
not_for: End-users without a GitHub Copilot subscription — all extension users must be active Copilot subscribers
Standalone chatbot experiences outside the GitHub Copilot interface
High-volume API access patterns — Extensions are invoked synchronously during chat sessions, not for batch processing
Non-developer audiences unfamiliar with the Copilot Chat UX and @mention invocation pattern
best_when: Your target users are developers already using GitHub Copilot who need contextual access to an external service without switching tools.
avoid_when: Your users don't have Copilot subscriptions, you need a general-purpose chatbot deployment, or setup complexity (GitHub App + OAuth) outweighs the benefit.

auth.notes: Extensions authenticate via a GitHub App with OAuth. When a user invokes the extension, Copilot passes an OAuth token scoped to the user's GitHub context. The extension validates the token against the GitHub OIDC endpoint before responding. Token scopes are requested during GitHub App setup and must be minimized per GitHub's review requirements.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Extensions API itself is free for developers to build against. Users require GitHub Copilot Individual ($10/month), Business ($19/user/month), or Enterprise ($39/user/month) subscriptions.
pricing.paid_tiers: No direct cost to extension developers beyond their own infrastructure. Listing in the GitHub Marketplace is free; premium extensions can set their own pricing.
pricing.notes: The developer cost is your own compute and any LLM API calls your extension makes. The end-user cost is their existing Copilot subscription. GitHub handles user billing and authentication; you handle your backend.

performance.rate_limits: No documented per-extension rate limits from GitHub's side. Extensions must respond within a streaming timeout (~30 seconds) or the request fails. Backend rate limits are determined by the extension's own infrastructure.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: The Extensions API is relatively new and documentation gaps exist. Error handling within the SSE streaming protocol is not well-documented. Extension errors surface to users as generic Copilot errors without developer-visible detail. GitHub App setup errors are clear; extension invocation errors are less so.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Chat interactions are stateless from the extension's perspective — each invocation is an independent request. No idempotency mechanism provided.
known_agent_gotchas: GitHub App creation and OAuth callback setup is required before any extension testing — no lightweight API-key-only path
Extensions must implement SSE (Server-Sent Events) streaming — non-streaming JSON responses are not supported
OIDC token validation must be implemented correctly in the extension backend — skipping it is a security vulnerability
Extensions are invoked only when users type @your-extension-name — discoverability depends on user education
GitHub Copilot policy settings can block extensions org-wide — extension availability may be restricted by admins
Extension review and Marketplace listing requires GitHub approval with security review — not self-serve
Context passed to extensions is limited to the current conversation; no automatic access to repo code or GitHub context without explicit API calls

security_score_components.security_notes: OAuth + GitHub App authentication is a robust security model when implemented correctly. OIDC token validation is mandatory and well-documented as a security requirement. GitHub reviews extensions before Marketplace listing. The main risk is extension developers misimplementing token validation — GitHub provides verification helpers in official SDKs. Scope granularity is good — OAuth scopes must be declared and minimized.
